rows.title: Livestock types

text_1.eng: Livestock includes domesticated animals raised for food, labor, and other agricultural products.

rows.text_1: Livestock includes domesticated animals raised for food, labor, and other agricultural products.

rows.title: Livestock keeping

text_1.eng: Livestock keeping, also known as animal husbandry, is the practice of raising animals.

image_text.eng:
Livestock kept are:

 * Cattle, Poultry, sheep, goat, 
 * Pigs, Rabbits, and Donkeys

rows.text_1: Livestock keeping, also known as animal husbandry, is the practice of raising animals.
rows.image_text:
Livestock kept are:

 * Cattle, Poultry, sheep, goat, 
 * Pigs, Rabbits, and Donkeys

section_1_text.eng: You may have milk or animals but no reliable market.

section_1_extra_text.eng: Add value to milk (e.g., make mala / sour milk).

section_2_text.eng: Animals get sick easily, leading to low production.

section_2_extra_text.eng:
* Vaccinate early.
 * Deworm regularly.
* Maintain hygiene to reduce infections.
 * Call a vet when needed. 
 * Isolate sick animals. 
 * Cull animals that cannot recover. 
 * Remove ticks by hand or spray using dips.
 * Use safe local herbs (e.g., aloe vera, blackjack and tithonia) 
 * Provide good feeding to boost immunity.

rows.section_1_text: You may have milk or animals but no reliable market.
rows.section_1_extra_text: Add value to milk (e.g., make mala / sour milk).
rows.section_2_text: Animals get sick easily, leading to low production.
rows.section_2_extra_text:
* Vaccinate early.
 * Deworm regularly.
* Maintain hygiene to reduce infections.
 * Call a vet when needed. 
 * Isolate sick animals. 
 * Cull animals that cannot recover. 
 * Remove ticks by hand or spray using dips.
 * Use safe local herbs (e.g., aloe vera, blackjack and tithonia) 
 * Provide good feeding to boost immunity.

section_1_extra_text.eng:
* Plant fodder like Brachiaria, Vetiver, and Napier.
 * Keep only the number of animals you can comfortably feed.
 * Prepare fermented feeds

section_2_text.eng: Poor feeding and breeding affect growth.

section_2_extra_text.eng:
* Use improved feeds for each growth stage.
* Monitor animals for disease signs.
* Observe breeding behavior (e.g., heat signs).

rows.section_1_extra_text:
* Plant fodder like Brachiaria, Vetiver, and Napier.
 * Keep only the number of animals you can comfortably feed.
 * Prepare fermented feeds
rows.section_2_text: Poor feeding and breeding affect growth.
rows.section_2_extra_text:
* Use improved feeds for each growth stage.
* Monitor animals for disease signs.
* Observe breeding behavior (e.g., heat signs).

section_1_text.eng: Animals may be stolen or attacked.

section_1_extra_text.eng:
* Keep dogs
 * Fence the homestead.
 * Install security lights.
 * Build stronger livestock shelters.
 * Report issues through local authorities (chief, nyumba kumi).

section_2_text.eng: Poor or weak animal shelters cause stress and disease.

section_2_extra_text.eng:
* Build proper shade for animals.
 * Maintain good hygiene and sanitation.
 * Use simple portable structures (e.g., masera/otete for chicks)
 * Repair damaged shelters.

rows.section_1_text: Animals may be stolen or attacked.
rows.section_1_extra_text:
* Keep dogs
 * Fence the homestead.
 * Install security lights.
 * Build stronger livestock shelters.
 * Report issues through local authorities (chief, nyumba kumi).
rows.section_2_text: Poor or weak animal shelters cause stress and disease.
rows.section_2_extra_text:
* Build proper shade for animals.
 * Maintain good hygiene and sanitation.
 * Use simple portable structures (e.g., masera/otete for chicks)
 * Repair damaged shelters.

section_1_text.eng: Lack of pasture and water causes poor animal nutrition.

section_1_extra_text.eng:
* Store feeds early (e.g., hay). 
 * Dig a well or borehole for water.
 * Give mineral supplements (e.g., salt lick).
 * Plant more fodder crops.

rows.section_1_text: Lack of pasture and water causes poor animal nutrition.
rows.section_1_extra_text:
* Store feeds early (e.g., hay). 
 * Dig a well or borehole for water.
 * Give mineral supplements (e.g., salt lick).
 * Plant more fodder crops.

rows.title: Dewormers

section_1_text.eng: Crush and soak in water for 30–35 minutes to make a deworming solution.

section_2_text.eng:
* It is used as both internal & external parasite control
 * To use it, soak dried Tephrosia powder in water in a 1:10 ratio overnight.
 * Strain and use the liquid

section_2_extra_text.eng: Internally: * Use 2 ml for adult birds * For chicks under 8 weeks use 1 ml Externally: * Dip birds in solution twice a week. (Do NOT dip incubating or laying birds.) * For laying/incubating birds, mix dry Tephrosia powder or leaves into bedding.

rows.section_1_text: Crush and soak in water for 30–35 minutes to make a deworming solution.
rows.section_2_text:
* It is used as both internal & external parasite control
 * To use it, soak dried Tephrosia powder in water in a 1:10 ratio overnight.
 * Strain and use the liquid
rows.section_2_extra_text: Internally: * Use 2 ml for adult birds * For chicks under 8 weeks use 1 ml Externally: * Dip birds in solution twice a week. (Do NOT dip incubating or laying birds.) * For laying/incubating birds, mix dry Tephrosia powder or leaves into bedding.

rows.title: Dewormers

section_1_text.eng: Dry, crush to powder, and mix small amounts into feed.

section_2_text.eng: Use crushed pawpaw leaves (male or female) for coccidiosis (blood in droppings)

section_2_extra_text.eng:
* Soak in water, ferment with molasses for 3 days.
 * Mix with drinking water at 1 part mixture: 2 parts water.
 * Hanging the leaves also helps, but is less effective.

rows.section_1_text: Dry, crush to powder, and mix small amounts into feed.
rows.section_2_text: Use crushed pawpaw leaves (male or female) for coccidiosis (blood in droppings)
rows.section_2_extra_text:
* Soak in water, ferment with molasses for 3 days.
 * Mix with drinking water at 1 part mixture: 2 parts water.
 * Hanging the leaves also helps, but is less effective.

text_1.eng: Some natural management practices supporting herbal remedies are:

section_1_text.eng: Hang inside the poultry house or use as bedding.

section_2_text.eng: Hang in the house or spray extract.

section_3_text.eng: Burn mixture near the poultry house to repel pests.

rows.text_1: Some natural management practices supporting herbal remedies are:
rows.section_1_text: Hang inside the poultry house or use as bedding.
rows.section_2_text: Hang in the house or spray extract.
rows.section_3_text: Burn mixture near the poultry house to repel pests.

section_1_text.eng: Dry and place in incubating/brooding houses, or plant around the poultry house (rosemary works too)

section_2_text.eng: Prepare as a tea for drinking water or dry and mix into feed; hanging inside the house helps the air quality

rows.section_1_text: Dry and place in incubating/brooding houses, or plant around the poultry house (rosemary works too)
rows.section_2_text: Prepare as a tea for drinking water or dry and mix into feed; hanging inside the house helps the air quality

text_1.eng: Some ways of ensuring proper poultry health through general management practices and environmental sanitation are:

section_1_text.eng: Keep vegetation short around poultry houses to reduce hiding spots for pests.

section_2_text.eng: Clean houses regularly to prevent disease.

rows.text_1: Some ways of ensuring proper poultry health through general management practices and environmental sanitation are:
rows.section_1_text: Keep vegetation short around poultry houses to reduce hiding spots for pests.
rows.section_2_text: Clean houses regularly to prevent disease.

section_1_text.eng: Different animals (poultry, cows, goats) need different feed.

section_2_text.eng: Chicks, growers, layers, calves, and adult cows all eat differently.

section_3_text.eng: Animals kept for meat or eggs require different nutrients.

section_4_text.eng: Sick, pregnant, and breeding animals need special attention.

section_5_text.eng: Male animals generally eat more than females

rows.section_1_text: Different animals (poultry, cows, goats) need different feed.
rows.section_2_text: Chicks, growers, layers, calves, and adult cows all eat differently.
rows.section_3_text: Animals kept for meat or eggs require different nutrients.
rows.section_4_text: Sick, pregnant, and breeding animals need special attention.
rows.section_5_text: Male animals generally eat more than females

text_1.eng: These are types of livestock feeds that can be prepared at home using locally available farm and simple household materials, mainly aimed at reducing the cost of feeding livestock

text_2.eng: Ensure to watch videos at the 'Practical Section' on how to make these feeds and the ingredents used.

rows.text_1: These are types of livestock feeds that can be prepared at home using locally available farm and simple household materials, mainly aimed at reducing the cost of feeding livestock
rows.text_2: Ensure to watch videos at the 'Practical Section' on how to make these feeds and the ingredents used.

rows.title: Fermented Feeds

text_1.eng: Fermented feeds are prepared without air (anaerobic conditions) using microorganisms. This process improves nutrient absorption and digestibility.

text_2.eng:
We use locally available materials, following the Go–Grow–Glow ratio:
 * 70% Go (Carbohydrates)
 * 25% Grow (Proteins)
 * 5% Glow (Vitamins & Minerals)

rows.text_1: Fermented feeds are prepared without air (anaerobic conditions) using microorganisms. This process improves nutrient absorption and digestibility.
rows.text_2:
We use locally available materials, following the Go–Grow–Glow ratio:
 * 70% Go (Carbohydrates)
 * 25% Grow (Proteins)
 * 5% Glow (Vitamins & Minerals)

rows.title: Fermented Feeds

section_1_text.eng:
* Cooked rice (for indigenous microbes)
 * Organic manure (microbe starter)
 * Poultry microbes / EM
 * Banana stems & leaves
 * Weeds: blackjack, gallant soldier, wandering jew
 * Napier grass, arrowroot leaves, pumpkin leaves

rows.section_1_text:
* Cooked rice (for indigenous microbes)
 * Organic manure (microbe starter)
 * Poultry microbes / EM
 * Banana stems & leaves
 * Weeds: blackjack, gallant soldier, wandering jew
 * Napier grass, arrowroot leaves, pumpkin leaves

rows.title: Fermented Feeds

section_1_text.eng:
* Improves birds’ gut health.
 * Lowers feeding costs by using local materials and weeds.
 * Fermentation starts digestion, making feed easier to digest and nutrients easier to absorb.
 * Adds probiotics that boost immunity.
 * Preserves feed during seasons of plenty for use in dry periods.
 * Reduces anti-nutritional factors in certain ingredients (e.g., fodder trees, soya), thereby increasing nutrient availability.

rows.section_1_text:
* Improves birds’ gut health.
 * Lowers feeding costs by using local materials and weeds.
 * Fermentation starts digestion, making feed easier to digest and nutrients easier to absorb.
 * Adds probiotics that boost immunity.
 * Preserves feed during seasons of plenty for use in dry periods.
 * Reduces anti-nutritional factors in certain ingredients (e.g., fodder trees, soya), thereby increasing nutrient availability.

rows.title: Homemade Poultry Feed

text_1.eng: It is feed you formulate and mix yourself to meet the nutritional needs of your animals.

image_text.eng:
* The main purpose of homemade feed is to:

 * Reduce feeding costs*
 * Use locally available resources*
 * Control the quality of feed given to animals*

rows.text_1: It is feed you formulate and mix yourself to meet the nutritional needs of your animals.

 * Reduce feeding costs*
 * Use locally available resources*

rows.title: Silage Making

text_1.eng: Silage making is the process of preserving fresh green plant material such as maize, sorghum, or grass by creating conditions that allow it to ferment naturally in the absence of air.

text_2.eng: It is a method of preserving animal feed during seasons of plenty, so it can be used when feed is scarce.

rows.text_1: Silage making is the process of preserving fresh green plant material such as maize, sorghum, or grass by creating conditions that allow it to ferment naturally in the absence of air.
rows.text_2: It is a method of preserving animal feed during seasons of plenty, so it can be used when feed is scarce.
